The biggest mistake in trading I use to make is trying to catch every single move. 

Greed is a drug. When people see numbers go up, all they want is more. This market is one big casino in many ways. People always want more, no matter how much they already have.

It takes a lot of experience and error to truly understand your limits, to know when to exit.

Making money is easy; keeping it is the hard part. Sometimes you need to take a step back and realize this: take profit or the market will take it from you.

Likewise, selling early doesn’t make you weak. I see so many people obsessed with catching the exact top, when in reality, the real veterans, the OGs, have already been selling for months. They don’t try to time perfection; they scale out when risk appetite shifts.

Because in the end, trading isn’t about predicting every move, it’s about surviving long enough to capitalize on the right ones.

$BTC Weekly Market Update (1W Focus)

On the LTF, price action has been quite choppy. For this update, I’ll be focused solely on the HTF (1W TF).

Overall structure remains technically bullish despite the noise and panic. To get proper context, let’s rewind and review recent price action before mapping out possible future scenarios.

Recent 3–4 Week Overview:
We saw a major liquidation wick to the downside, the largest liquidation event in history, with rumors that several big institutional players were caught in the move (unconfirmed, of course). After that, price pushed up to the 0.5/0.618 retracement of the range on a retest, reaching 115K. From there, it reversed, filling over half the wick of the crash candle. The following candle reclaimed the range and pushed back to the monthly open with a strong close, which under normal circumstances should’ve signaled continuation.

Instead, we opened high into FOMC, swept the monthly open with a slight deviation, and dropped back to the 1W POC and Quarterly level.

Market Context
This recent sequence shows that price has struggled to gain acceptance above the monthly open and the key pivot just below the VAH. We’ve now had three clear rejections from this zone, along with a break of the 1W trendline. Price has returned to the same quarterly lows again, and given the context and market structure, it looks like a deeper sweep could be on the table, something most aren’t expecting after repeated rejections and clear lower highs on the weekly. Its important to understand that if supply exceeds demand, we need to attract buyers. You only attract big buyers at lower levels. 

If we lose the 107–108K range, that opens the door for sweeps into 102–101K, with possible deviation into the untested imbalance below 100K around 97–98K.
If BTC manages to hold 107–108K, we could see a push back to 110–112K LTF levels. 

But for true continuation, we must flip the monthly open. Multiple hunts above it with no sustained bid suggest distribution, which further supports the idea that we need to test lower demand zones to attract buyers and weaken overhead supply. The area of interest remains the grey box if 107–108K is lost.

Sentiment
Market sentiment is clearly fearful, everyone’s screaming that it’s over. Eventually, the overall narrative will be right. We’ve seen the overall narrative be correct in a uptrend, so the same logic applies in a downtrend. Normally, I like counter-trading extreme sentiment, but the current structure tells another story.

Failure to flip key areas, compression below major zones, and curling averages all show fading momentum. That means we need fresh demand, demand likely found at lower levels.

That’s why I’ve taken 4 shorts recently (all publicly shared), and all of them played out. Longs remain risky in this environment.

The Good & The Bad

The good news: BTC is still holding above the quarterly open.

The bad news: it’s failed 3 times to reclaim the monthly open. The sweep above 115K likely served to take out shorts, which it did effectively.

Outlook
For bullish continuation, we need a strong weekly close above 115K. Until that happens, lower levels remain the primary target.

Wanted to share my thoughts regarding this $BTC

We just witnessed one of the largest liquidation events in market history. Normally, such events are seen as strong buy signals during a bull market, they wash out leverage and reset sentiment.

But this time feels different to me. The scale of the liquidations wiped out a number of key market makers and liquidity providers. That kind of structural damage raises real questions about balance sheet health and the ecosystem’s ability to absorb shocks going forward.

BTC has now been ranging above $100K for roughly 3 months, consistent with the duration of most ranges in this cycle (with the exception of the $73K–$50K range, which lasted around six months). One of these ranges will eventually break, either up or down.

From a pure market structure standpoint, this looks more like distribution than accumulation to me. The recent drop from $123K to $108K on spot (with perps wicking to around $102K) is notable, but it doesn’t yet qualify as a true flush. If you’ve been in these markets as long as me, you know that kind of move isn’t necessarily “the” capitulation. I think we still need a deeper, more visceral correction, something that truly resets positioning and sentiment for BTC.

I could be wrong, of course. But I’ve been publicly short since $123K, and my bias remains for lower levels. Given how extended borrowing and margin levels are right now, both sitting near ATH levels... it seems far more probable that we see sub-$100K before new highs.

Ask yourself: since when does it make sense for retail to be fully positioned ahead of rate cuts and the end of QT? When has the market ever rewarded the obvious narrative?

Just my two thoughts. 👻

The Recent $BTC Liquidation Cascade Exposed the Structural Flaws in Centralized Derivatives Platforms.

The recent BTC liquidation cascade, when price nuked from $123K down to $102K... perfectly highlights how structurally skewed this industry is toward exchange-side profit protection. Once again, we saw Auto-Deleveraging (ADL) events across Bybit, Binance, and Hyperliquid.

In theory, ADL mechanisms are meant to protect market integrity during extreme volatility. In practice, they’re a systemic wealth transfer, where profitable traders’ PnL is forcibly reduced to offset the losses of overleveraged counterparties.

Imagine this: you nail the short from $123K, execute perfectly, and watch your position print a 6-figure unrealized profit. Then, out of nowhere, you get auto-deleveraged, your winning position closed prematurely at breakeven or worse, because the exchange can’t internally match liquidation orders fast enough. That’s not "risk management." That’s counterparty reallocation masked as stability.

When cascading liquidations occur, the system attempts to offload bankrupt positions at market. If liquidity dries up and no one’s willing to take the other side, the exchange eats a negative account balance. 

Pretty much, this is absorbed by the platform’s insurance fund, which exists precisely for these scenarios. But when losses exceed that buffer... which clearly happened during this drop, the burden gets passed on to winning traders via ADL.

And the worst part? Exchanges frame this as if they’re "protecting the ecosystem" or "compensating users out of their own pockets." In reality, those funds are your realized profits being reallocated to cover someone else’s liquidation shortfall.

Meanwhile, @BitunixOfficial stood out as one of the only platforms that handled this correctly, absorbing the losses entirely in-house instead of clawing back from profitable accounts. That’s how risk management should work: the exchange assumes operational responsibility, not its users. Bitunix comepensated the people who lost under me + let the winners win.

If you value fair execution, true counterparty separation, and no ADL theft of PnL, I’d seriously consider trading under me on Bitunix instead of the usual platforms. 

The recent event proved one thing beyond doubt, on most of these "top-tier" exchanges, the house always wins. Just DM me if you want to setup a deal.

$BTC | IMPORTANT

I made this chart a few months ago to show how “news” tends to correlate with Bitcoin’s price action.

Since Bitcoin’s creation, we’ve seen a clear pattern: when a bull run ends, the market becomes flooded with bad news. During the bull phase, though, traders become conditioned to “buy the dip,” as negative news often mark local bottoms within an ongoing uptrend.

This conditioning creates a powerful emotional bias, traders begin to associate fear and bad news with opportunity. Every correction feels like a chance to profit, not a warning sign. But when the cycle finally shifts into a bear market, that same mindset turns dangerous. The instinct to “buy the dip” keeps traders trapped, buying into weakness while the broader trend has already reversed.

What once felt like confidence quickly turns into denial, frustration, and eventually capitulation. The market doesn’t just test portfolios, it tests emotional resilience.

But in bear markets, it’s the opposite, bad news usually confirms or accelerates the downtrend, rather than signaling a buying opportunity.

Right now, we seem to be at that same stage again with tariffs and trade war headlines. The market’s showing indecision, and Trump keeps flipping his stance. 

My view? This is just the start of the next wave of “bad news”,  and I think it’s only getting started.

This is the part where everyone is secretly longing $BTC in the background but won’t post about it unless it actually plays out. 😂

If it doesn’t, nobody will ever know. Everyone’s obviously not short, just stuck in complacency. I repeat, I am not building any longs at all.

If we completely set aside fundamentals, such as (news/tariffs) and focus purely on market structure from a technical perspective, we’re approaching a key area.

The purple box marks the 0.5–0.618 retracement of the entire range. After forming a SFP (swing failure pattern) at the new ATH, PA below this zone remains bearish. We’re currently testing both the quarterly and monthly opens, so some deviation or short-term “fuckery” above them is possible, but the broader idea still holds.

As long as price stays below the purple box, the monthly lows around 108K are likely to be tested. Since we’re in the lower half of the range, continuation requires price to fully reclaim and hold above 114–116K for any real chance of printing a new ATH.

If the purple box is flipped into support, the next key zone to watch is 119.5K, a prior S/R level of significance. A clean break above that would likely lead to a new all-time high. Again, this analysis is purely technical.

Stop comparing Covid-19 Crash to Now. $BTC

I keep seeing people compare this week’s crash to the COVID-19 crash which is a complete misread of the cycle. These accounts have zero grasp on macro or BTC structure. Just engagement farming.

They’re comparing similar price drops in completely different environments.

Here’s the reality:
The COVID crash happened pre-halving, right after the bear market bottom - BTC was just about to enter into a bull run.

This current selloff? It comes after a 750% rally, at a major swing high, late in the cycle. Totally different time, totally different macro.

COVID Macro (2020):
• Fed cut rates to 0%
• Trillions in QE (“money printer go brrr”)
• Weak USD, liquidity flood
• Risk-on mania > BTC $4K > $69K
• Ultra-dovish macro backdrop > explosive upside

Tariff Crash (2025):
• Fed still above 5%
• QT ongoing, no new QE
• Tariffs = inflationary > keeps Fed hawkish
• USD strong, yields high, liquidity tight
• Restrictive macro backdrop > risk-off pressure

Bottom line:
You can’t compare a pre-halving, liquidity-fueled setup (COVID) to a late-cycle, liquidity-tight phase (now).

We’re much closer to the start of a Bitcoin bear phase, not another parabolic leg. Macro + cycle both say: sideways-to-down bias, not a parabolic run.

And yes, liquidations exceed COVID levels but that’s just market structure evolving: more leverage, more liquidity, more traders.

But high liquidations ≠ bullish reversal if the macro doesn’t support it.

Stop listening to larps.

$BTC | Cycles Update, My Plan

Many of you already know that I currently lean on the bearish side rather than the bullish side. I’ve shared several updates explaining my reasoning, but this will be the most comprehensive one yet. In this post, I’ll walk you through my full outlook and plan.

There’s always talk of a "super cycle," and narratives like that appear every single time. But if we strip away the noise, markets are and always have been, governed by macroeconomic cycles. 

These macro shifts aren’t just economic, they carry deep psychological weight. You see, Bitcoin was created to thrive in uncertainty, not in certainty.
It was born from chaos, a response to distrust in the system, not comfort within it.

However, most retail participants fail to grasp this. Market behavior moves in emotional waves that mirror these macro conditions, creating the same repeating patterns of accumulation, markup, distribution, and markdown time after time. However, there are always a few who try to outsmart the market, convinced they see what others don’t, that they know better, that this time will be different.

The market is, at its core, a machine engineered to profit from human emotion. It thrives on exploiting fear, greed, and impatience. And because human psychology doesn’t change, I don’t believe this cycle will be any different from the ones before it. Every time, people convince themselves that "this time is different," yet the structure of the market, and the emotions that drive it, remain the same.

The dollar’s value also moves in predictable cycles. It rises and falls in response to the Federal Reserve’s tightening and easing policies, as well as broader liquidity trends. However, the idea of an imminent dollar collapse is unrealistic. Structural devaluation happens slowly, over time, through sustained macro transitions, not overnight. This is key, because as liquidity tightens and the dollar strengthens, risk assets like Bitcoin typically face downward pressure.

All of these factors together form the foundation of my bearish outlook. Statistically, we’re entering the late stages of this current cycle. The data, probabilities, and timing all point to exhaustion rather than continuation. That’s why I’ve opened a 2.5x–3x leverage short using cross margin.

To be clear, this is not a reckless position. It represents only 10–15% of my overall portfolio, and my liquidation level, approximately 40% above the current price of $123,000. Liquidation on this swing sits at $170,000-$180,000. Based on my analysis, I view a 30–50% correction as far more likely than a continued parabolic move to those levels. My risk is defined, and I’m comfortable with it.

I understand why people ask, "Why go against the trend?" or "Why not wait for confirmation?" But trading the end of a cycle is not about following the crowd, it’s about positioning ahead of where sentiment turns. 

Capturing major reversals requires a strategy most traders are unwilling to use. Cross margin allows me to absorb volatility without emotional reaction, something retail traders often struggle alot with. Institutions and market makers use similar approaches because they operate with conviction, not emotion.

If I’m right, I’ll earn from both funding fees and the downside move, all while increasing my BTC holdings as the dollar strengthens. If I’m wrong, I’ll lose 10–15% of my capital, acceptable within my framework. Trading is about trusting your system and respecting your probabilities.

If Bitcoin somehow pushes into the $170,000–$180,000 range, that would imply a structural shift, perhaps even a break from the diminishing returns model that has defined prior cycles. But my strategy is built on data, not hope. It’s based on technical and macro confluence, not speculative narratives. I have also been 90% right this cycle on the HTF & my track record speaks for itself.

I’m currently net short and building this position gradually on a dedicated sub-account. For those who share my outlook, I recommend isolating no more than 10% of your portfolio for a similar structure: use low leverage, stay on cross margin, and let the liquidation define your risk.

I’ve used this same system at $54K, $76K, and $90K during the bull market, and it served its purpose. Now, given current conditions, I believe a correction toward $60K–$70K over the next 6–9 months is the most probable outcome.

This is a long-term swing trade, not a short-term play. Unless the account is liquidated, I intend to hold through next year 6-9 months.

I hope this helps clarify my reasoning and why I’m positioned this way. Stay disciplined, stay patient, and trust your system.

God bless. My swing short is open. 🤝

My opinions on a Super Cycle.

A lot of people believe that $BTC will experience a “super cycle” this time around because gold & legacy assets are exploding.

I disagree. I think we’ll only see a true super cycle for Bitcoin once gold enters a multi-year bear market, something that has happened several times in the past after major movements upwards. 

This cycle has been unlike any other, BTC/XAU remains below its ATH, and gold is significantly outperforming Bitcoin. This has never happened before. So...

When gold underperforms, capital will rotate into BTC. However, I think that timing aligns more with the next cycle, not this one. BTC has already risen about 750%, with 3 years of up only. Since ETFs launched at $38K, we’re up roughly 300%.

I don’t believe BTC is lagging, it’s more that supply currently outweighs demand. If the price drops 10–20%, we’ll likely see ETF outflows accelerate, triggering a natural domino effect similar to how strong inflows initially pushed the price up.

That would also mean the dollar bounces back after being down 13% year to date. 

Simply put: Dollar strengthens, gold declines, and Bitcoin typically shows similar weakness. But once gold enters its multi-year bear cycle, BTC will explode & have its first de-correlation from the 4 year cycles.

These are my current thoughts & I can be wrong.

What will happen to altcoins? 

Here’s how I see it:

From 2017–2020 we experienced the altcoin mania. ICOs, DeFi, and L2 projects all exploded, while BTCs market cap was still relatively small, around $20–30B. Because Bitcoins market cap was so small, altcoins were able to launch with inflated valuations, pulling BTC dominance (BTC.D) down to ~35%.

However, that dynamic has shifted completely. Bitcoin is the only asset that truly matters, especially as adoption accelerates. With ETFs, corporations & governments buying, $BTC's market cap will continue to grow. Meanwhile, altcoins will lose even more traction because they cannot keep pace. New coins flood the market every day, diluting liquidity and making sustainable growth impossible.

As $BTC strengthens, capital flows consolidate into it, leaving altcoins with smaller caps, lower dominance, and increasingly illiquid markets. This is why BTC.D will gradually rise toward 70–80% over the next 5–15 years. Bitcoin becomes the safe, regulated, institutional-grade asset, while altcoins all end up as slow rugs.

Crucially, this also means Bitcoin won’t experience the same kinds of catastrophic drawdowns we saw in the past. As regulations mature and adoption deepens, downside volatility will shrink. The era of extreme BTC crashes will decrease. 

Many still think the current cycle isn’t over because altcoins have underperformed. In reality, that underperformance is a feature of this transition. It’s not that the cycle is lagging, it’s that altcoins are being left behind. Over time, most will effectively become slow-motion rugs as liquidity dries up.

This isn’t a popular view, but it’s the truth. And it’s how the BTC.D will ultimately play out in the long run.

Some context regarding the $USDT printing. 

$BTC doesn’t react to liquidity in, it reacts to timing, context, and composition of that liquidity. When the Fed cuts rates or prints money near macro bottoms or early in a bull cycle, fresh capital enters a hungry market, driving prices higher as risk on sentiment accelerates.

But liquidity arriving late in a cycle often coincides with distribution, where exiting positions absorb the inflow so “new” liquidity doesn’t push BTC higher; it’s just replacing capital leaving the market. Which is usually why tops form when people least expect it.

When the Fed cuts rates or prints money, it injects liquidity, but the impact on BTC depends entirely on the macro cycle, whether the capital enters a market ready to absorb it and push prices higher, or arrives late when it simply replaces exiting positions, limiting upside and signaling a potential top.

As you know, billions of $USDT has been printed with little immediate impact on price. Only time will tell how it plays out, but the context and timing of when this liquidity enters the market are important. That’s why I’m taking a cautious approach and building low-leverage swing shorts.

New liquidity doesn't always mean price goes up.

Time for some facts $BTC

If you strip this cycle down to Bitcoin alone, the story is pretty simple: BTC rallied from $16K to $124K, a 700% move. On the chart alone, without looking at ETH or alts, most people would call this topped or close to topped. The only reason there’s hesitation now is because certain sectors have underperformed, which leaves bagholders clinging to the idea of "more upside."

This cycle has been very different from 2017 or even 2020. Back then, the 2019–2020 bubble was all about utility projects, staking plays, yield tokens, and DeFi protocols. That era birthed things like Aave, Compound, Uniswap, and the whole “money lego” movement.

This time? The spotlight has COMPLETELY shifted. The mania has revolved around memecoins on Solana. In other words "the trenches" as traders call it. Countless tokens have printed 50x–100x moves. 

On top of that, early in the cycle we saw monster rallies from selective projects: Injective, Optimism, Arbitrum, and other L2s. And of course, the biggest headline act was Solana itself,exploding from $9 to $300.

So while this cycle feels “selective,” the truth is there’s been no shortage of opportunities. If you didn’t make money, it’s likely because you didn’t ride the narratives. The biggest difference this cycle is liquidity fragmentation. In past cycles, only a handful of new coins launched, so capital flowed into a concentrated set of assets, fueling mega rallies. 

Today, thousands of new tokens hit the market every day, constantly draining liquidity from existing projects. Instead of capital concentrating on a few coins, it’s scattered across thousands, diluting gains and making it feel like nothing is pumping, even though opportunities are still there for those who rotate wisely.

The psychology here is important. A lot of people think: "My bags haven’t pumped, so the cycle isn’t over... BTC has to go way higher for my coins to catch up." But that’s not how it works. Bitcoin doesn’t have to go to $200K just so your illiquid alt finally wakes up. BTC already did its job.

At this point, BTC is showing late cycle behavior. Sure, if we can consolidate above $100K, maybe there’s an extra +10–15% left in the tank. But the bulk of the run is behind us. That doesn’t mean altcoins are finished though. Historically, alts often stage exit pumps even after BTC cools off, because capital rotates down the risk curve.

The bottom line: 
This cycle wasn’t about broad based "everything pumps" like 2017-2020. It’s been about narrative surfing. Solana memes, selective L2s, and a handful of asymmetric trades. If you missed them, that’s not because Bitcoin “owes” you another leg to $200K. It’s because you didn’t pivot with the market.

$BTC | Weekly and Monthly Market Report

All levels were identified in advance, all analysis was shared publicly, and everything that unfolded has been purely technical and within expectations. The monthly open became a natural target after the rally into FOMC, where markets had priced in rate cuts and the overall narrative was clearly bullish.

Whats interesting is that the majority of this app were telling you "everyone is short". In reality, I was one of the few highlighting that the market was net long going into FOMC. This was obvious because everyone was waiting for rate cuts. There is still this illusion that Q4 always delivers a strong rally, but eventually the cycle will shift, and a Q4 rally will fail. That is why I’ve maintained a cautious stance.

I noted weeks ago that we have a large exhaustion gap at 116.5K. It took time, but the gap was eventually filled. I also highlighted in a separate post that whenever BTC creates these types of gaps, it often signals a shift in market structure and trend. We saw this both in 2021 and this cycle, when BTC left a major gap from 101K to 91K, filled it, and then reversed down to 74K in the following months.

Now that BTC has filled the imbalance and rejected immediately while the market was heavily long into FOMC, structure remains extremely bearish below this level. Bulls and larger players will only look to accumulate long positions once 116.5K is reclaimed, but so far the rejection from this zone has been decisive. This is why I called attention to shorts around 116K.

Because of this, I’ve been extremely cautious, avoiding swing long positions. For weeks and months I’ve repeated that this is not the time to be aggressively bullish or chasing swing longs. The market is too close to the end of the cycle based on mathematical models.

At present, BTC is retesting the monthly open after a 6–7% rally earlier this month. The current rejection is not attractive, but as we approach the monthly close, it is important to outline key scenarios.

If BTC fails to hold the monthly open, lower timeframes will likely wick down to the monthly low.

If the monthly low does not hold, we likely push to 104–105K, creating significant market panic. This level could provide long opportunities, but with broken structure and a bearish LTF trend, it is risky to attempt longs without confirmation.

If 104–105K breaks, BTC will trade below 100K. I have been very clear for months: if the quarterly candle closes under 100K, we enter into a bear market. No ambiguity. I have also stated that I was selling most of my holdings and have been steadily scaling out of the market for months.

From a technical perspective, the market is not bullish. Fundamentally, one bullish factor has been Tether’s aggressive USDT printing, which in the past has fueled rallies. However, this is speculative, as we cannot know how the capital will be deployed. Importantly, there are currently more bearish technical signals than bullish ones. Lets move onto the bullsih scenario:

Bullish Scenario
If BTC holds the monthly open or monthly low, or even wicks into 105–106K to trigger panic, the first confirmation would be a reclaim of the prior ATH at 108–109K. Failure to reclaim that level signals acceptance into a lower range and sets up a direct move toward sub-100K levels.

If BTC trades near or slightly under 100K, I may attempt a long given the untested region. However, for a swing position, we would need to see a reclaim of bullish structure. A sustained breakdown below 100K sends us toward the yearly open at 93K. That level will eventually be tested, but the timing remains uncertain.

Current Market Context
BTC has liquidated $2.5B in longs (the largest in years) and erased $26B in options, maximizing pain across participants. Such widespread liquidations can sometimes mark bullish turning points, but there will eventually come a time when these liquidations precede even deeper downside.

So while heavy liquidations may support short-term longs if key levels hold, it is crucial to wait for structural confirmation rather than blindly chasing falling knives.

I have attached links to my prior $BTC updates for reference. Unlike much of the noise on this platform, my posts document a consistent thesis on the HTFs. 

My bias has not been bullish in this region, and my stance has remained cautious, based on data showing we are nearing a cycle peak. I am not permanently bearish or permanently bullish, just consistent in my market outlook when HTF signals are clear.

Didn’t expect this post to blow up... so im going to dig deeper into the $USDT topic

The beauty of this market is that everything is on-chain. Every Tether mint, burn, and transfer is fully traceable. What most people don’t realize is how powerful this information is if you know how to read it.  OGs and seasoned traders understand the signal, but very few can position around it.

Tether reported $5.7B in net profit just in Q1/Q2 this year. They’ve openly stated part of that comes from buying BTC and gold. Alongside Binance, Tether can single handedly influence $BTC's market price. Now, a billion dollars won’t move a multi trillion dollar asset directly... but here’s the thing: when Tether buys, when Binance buys, institutions and hedge funds follow. That cascade naturally pushes BTC higher.

This is why tracing Tether mints is almost like reading a code for the HTF movements. When the prints are aggressive, the playbook is usually the same: they flush leverage, take out long stops, and then gradually accumulate in the zones where retail panics. That’s why you don’t always see an immediate pump after a mint. Their execution is strategic. Price action is engineered to shake conviction before the real move develops. 

In short, Tether prints are a god tier signal, but only a select few know how to capitalize on it. I’ve been pointing this out for months & if you check the post below, every time I called out a major mint, the move eventually played. It just took time.

So when Tether mints multiple billions in USDT, it will get deployed, but not all at once. First, leverage gets hunted. That’s often where their real entries begin.

Unironically, when the macro shifts, Tether stops printing, and BTC ranges before having a correction. Again, this is visible on chain. 

It may sound cliché, but the reality is: Tether is printing billions in net profit. And if you want to understand what’s coming, you need to start tracking how they do it.

I present to you... The Binance Cartel.

They see everything. Trading on some tiny CEX? Doesn’t matter. Every order ultimately flows through the same network of Binance linked market makers, giving them a clear view of virtually every major position in the market.

Binance operates at giga scale, with aggregated visibility across order book depth, liquidity pools, and synthetic derivatives exposure. Every meaningful position is mapped, delta and gamma footprints traced, implied vol surfaces monitored, and every cluster of leverage + funding rate arbitrage. To them, the market is fully transparent and infinitely exploitable.

They sit at the top of the food chain. The are seeing not just price, but the full positioning across spot, futures, options, and perpetual contracts. Order flow, liquidity vacuums, stop-loss walls, margin call cascades, and gamma squeezes.

With this insight, they do not merely participate; they orchestrate. They engineer pinning dynamics, force liquidations through convexity exposure, manipulate skew to induce hedging flows, and exploit funding asymmetries. Every move they make is max pain.

To them, volatility is not chaos but a tool weaponized to extract capital from the many and funnel it into the hands of the few. Binance.

The truth is almost philosophical: markets are not mechanisms of fairness, but of predation. Every retail trade is a data point in a larger algorithm of extraction.

The majority are not players; they are the product. The Binance Cartel is real. $BTC

Not many people preach this, so I will:

If you’re trading, your focus shouldn’t be on trading 100 different assets. Your goal should be to master the rhythm of one asset. For me, that asset is $BTC. 

I’ve developed multiple strategies for both scalping and swinging, and a lot of the pivots and insights I share here come from the countless hours I’ve spent trading.

When you try to trade multiple assets, you run into problems: each one moves differently, has unique volatility, and reacts to levels in ways that may not be consistent. I’ve experienced this myself. Whenever I spread my attention across different assets, I usually end up taking a unnecessary loss. That’s why it’s crucial to pick one coin, stick with it, and master it.

- If BTC dips into 113.5K early in the week (Mon/Tue), you should be searching for continuation longs the rest of the week.

- If instead BTC pushes into 117–118K right out of the gate (Mon/Tue), I’ll be eyeing shorts.

Here’s why: at the start of a new weekly open, price typically sets either the high or the low of the wick. Observing how that forms early on often sets the tone for the rest of the week.

Lose 113.5K, and 111K is next (likely CPI-driven).

Hold above 113.5K, and with FOMC on the horizon, I’m watching resistance/support carefully. If strength holds, 119–120K is the upside target.

So based on the poll, 70% of you said you would long this chart. What you didn’t realize is that the chart is actually USDT.D, which moves opposite to BTC. That means if you’re longing this chart, you’re essentially expecting BTC to go down.

$BTC | Massive Cycles Update

I’ve made some bold claims and predictions recently, and today I want to walk through the reasoning behind them. However, its important to note that successful trading is about adaptability, shaping ideas, assessing odds, and letting the market itself decide which paths are valid, while knowing that conviction must always leave room for flexibility.

Right now, I am leaning toward the idea that the cycle top is either already in, or very close to forming. I recognize this is a bold statement and expect people to disagree with me. 

To be clear, I am not trying to pinpoint the exact top to the day. My focus is on protecting capital and identifying the broader window in which risk is elevated. If my timing is off by a few months, or if BTC pushes toward 130–140K before rolling over below 70K, that does not invalidate the thesis. The goal is to avoid buying long-term bags into exhaustion trying to catch the last 10-15%.

My track record of HTF predictions has been strong, but I am not here to boast... I am here to present my analysis.

Halving Cycles and Timing

Cycle 1: Topped 360 days post-halving
Cycle 2: Topped 518 days post-halving
Cycle 3: Topped 546 days post-halving

We are currently 497 days post-halving. If BTC follows the same rhythm, a top within the next two months would align with historical behavior. However, there is an important caveat: BTC already printed a new ATH pre-halving, something we’ve never seen in previous cycles. The ETF approval likely pulled demand forward, creating the possibility of a shorter cycle than the past three.

Bullish Confluences
• Historically, post-halving cycles have been lengthening. That would imply BTC still has time left before topping.
• Over $6B in USDT was printed in a week, signaling liquidity inflows.
• Rate cuts are typically seen as bullish for risk assets, even if past data shows mixed outcomes.
• Liquidity clusters remain above around 124–130K, which could act as a magnet.

Bearish Confluences
• BTC has printed a clear exhaustion gap, something that in past cycles has signaled a trend shift. In this cycle, the last time we formed a exhaustion gap at 98K... It resulted in a trend shift & push down to 74K. However, as seen from my examples in 2021, BTC formed 2 gaps. Both with similar characteristics & both exhaustion gaps. Shortly after... BTC pushed down 30-40%.
• Diminishing returns suggest BTC is nearing its peak, as each cycle delivers lower percentage gains than the last.
• The monthly chart has now printed six straight green candles, but bodies and wicks are shrinking - a classic sign of slowing momentum.
• Price is grinding higher on declining volume, while bearish divergences appear across multiple timeframes.

Scenarios Around Rate Cuts
The real wildcard is the market’s reaction to upcoming rate cuts. Since expectations are already priced in, I am observing two plausible scenarios:

Bullish Continuation: BTC dips into the announcement (bearish narrative) but reverses quickly on lower timeframes. This could trigger a final euphoric leg higher, aligning with extended post-halving timing and a top in the October–November window.

Or

Exhaustion Top: BTC initially reacts bullishly, but liquidity injections act as exit liquidity for larger players. In this case, rate cuts could ironically mark the catalyst for the cycle peak.

At present, the crowd leans heavily toward a bullish reaction. That positioning itself makes me cautious. A failure to reclaim levels above the exhaustion gap would support the broader correction thesis, with a possible move toward 100K.

Broader Outlook
I remain bearish on the high-timeframe trend. Since 2022 I’ve been consistently bullish and publicly called the cycle bottom in my discord, but this is not the moment to get caught up in hopium. Tops are not meant to be easy to time, and it is far more important to protect capital than chase fantasies of “this cycle is different.”

The ETF narrative is often misunderstood. BlackRock is not buying BTC directly... their clients are buying through the ETF, and BlackRock is collecting commissions. That is retail demand, not institutional conviction.

The only unique factor that could alter the cycle dynamics is miner economics. With costs above 90K, many miners are operating at a loss. If BTC fails to keep pace with rising mining costs, rigs may shut down, which would reduce miner selling pressure. 

Beyond that, the math and historical structure of cycles remain intact, leaving little reason to believe this cycle will diverge significantly.

Conclusion
The cycle is in its late stages. At best, there may be a few months of upside left, but the evidence leans toward caution. Whether BTC tops slightly higher or has already peaked, the math suggests we are much closer to the end than the beginning. Protecting capital should now be the priority. If I happen to be wrong on the local LTF so be it. But in a year from now, people will look back an realize just how right I was.

$BTC | Weekly Market Report

As always, when analyzing the charts, I like to observe previous price action, particularly what happened in the week prior. This helps me identify and map out key areas to watch next. So, let’s get started:

Last Week: 
BTC formed a CME gap at 116.6K. At the start of the week, we began pushing down, resulting in a retest of 108.6K. Shortly after, we pushed up to the weekly open at 113.5K, which I highlighted as a key area to watch in my last weekly report. BTC then rejected the weekly open and pushed all the way down to 107.3K, breaking the previous range lows at 112K.

Using this information, we can dissect the narrative, identify which liquidity was swept, and which liquidity is exposed. As always, the market follows liquidity.

Currently: 
We have a weekend CME gap at 107.8K, which remains a highly probable target. The monthly close is also approaching.

Predicted the March bottom, but where will $BTC top?

In this post, I’m laying out my current thoughts and how I’m viewing the remainder of this cycle.

Back in January, I made a clear call: “We will bottom in March & top in September.” Looking back, the March bottom played out exactly as anticipated, and now we’re heading straight into that September window.

So, what comes next?

First off, all of my analysis is time-based, not surface-level fractal pattern hunting. The real edge lies in what is not immediately visible on the chart. That said, forecasts must always adapt to macro developments and live price action. What I’ll share here is my latest outlook, and maybe it will turn out to be one of the most important posts you’ll read this cycle.

Initially, my call for a September top was rooted in astrological alignments and time-based factors. But I’m beginning to see an alternative scenario emerge, mainly because $ETH has yet to create a new ATH. I strongly believe ETH will break that milestone before a true bear market begins. That’s not the only reason, though.

If we look back at Bitcoin’s halving cycles since 2012, each one has been extending slightly. Right now, we’re sitting at Day 488 since the halving. The previous cycle topped at $69K on Day 548. If we apply that same logic here, BTC may still have 1.5 to 2 months of runway, suggesting this cycle could stretch just beyond the September target.

If that extension plays out, it points toward a late Q4 top instead of a September peak. That’s why I’m treating this period as a critical pivot and weighing multiple outcomes.

Here’s how I see the scenarios:

Scenario 1: BTC tops in September in line with time-based analysis and rolls straight into a bear market.

Scenario 2: BTC does not top in September. Instead, Q4 serves as a pivot into continuation with new highs into year-end. However, by EOY, institutional profit-taking kicks in, setting the stage for significant downside into Q1.

Both are highly plausible. Which one plays out will depend on how the next weeks of PA unfold. The more structure we see, the easier it will be to calculate the precise top in real-time rather than far in advance.

Back to my main point:

Buying $BTC now is like buying at $50K last cycle. You’ll likely see short-term profit, but you’ll almost certainly botch the exit if you don’t plan. That’s why I’ve been selling into strength and scaling out of my holdings.

At this stage, the maximum upside is shrinking. After a 700% rally from the bottom, chasing the last 10–25% makes little sense, especially when history shows bear markets erase 50–80% of gains. That means sub-$100K BTC is almost guaranteed, and a retest of $60K–$70K within a year is increasingly likely.

I’ve seen multiple cycles, taken hits, and learned from my mistakes. That’s why I make posts like this, not to sound bearish but to stress the importance of exiting intelligently, using time-based principles and historical context.

If I’m wrong and BTC runs to $140K+, that’s only ~25% from here. It is not worth the risk compared to the 500–700% I have already caught.

The takeaway? We are very close to the end. Yes, scam wicks can push us higher, especially if the September top doesn’t hold. But that does not mean it’s time to be bullish. It means it’s time to be disciplined and execute exits.

Because in markets, as in life:
Planning turns uncertainty into clarity.

As the weekly close approaches, here’s what I’m watching heading into next week.

Review of this week (BTC):
BTC reclaimed the monthly open around 115.7Kand pushed into the 120K liquidation zone highlighted last week. After setting a new ATH and running stops on shorts, BTC met a sharp rejection - which was expected. The market always seeks liquidity, and in this case, buy-side liquidity sat above after filling the imbalance near 112K earlier in the week.

Outlook for next week:
BTC has now reached a point of equilibrium within its range. We’ve already swept the 112K lows and taken out the ATH stops. So, where’s the next pool of liquidity?

I expect the 115.8K-114K zone to be tested next week.

However, considering we’ve already retraced nearly 7K from the 124K highs, late shorts are now vulnerable. This opens the door for a potential 120K retest as well.

The key is not to predict what comes first, but to react when price reaches these levels.

At the start of next week, two scenarios are most likely:

1.) A push toward 119.5K–120K to establish Monday’s high. Or...

2.) A sweep lower into the monthly open liquidations at the start of the week hunting longs.

From my perspective, there’s an even probability of hunting either side first. When probabilities sit around 50/50, the smartest move is to sit sidelined or monitor LTF structure for either continuation or a breakdown.

At the end of the day, the most important question in trading remains:
 
"Where is the liquidity?"

By studying candle closures, wicks, and bodies, you can read the market narrative: who got hunted, who’s trapped, and who’s exposed. I value posting this type of analysis more than trade setups, since it offers a clearer view of how I read the market.

Looking back, all of my weekly outlooks have hit their targets without a miss.

$BTC ┃Weekly Analysis Report

Observations
BTC has flipped the monthly open, which is technically bullish. Often, early in a new month, we see price wick up or down to form one side of the monthly candle’s wick, a move I like to call the monthly open trap.

On the liquidity side, 2W BTC liquidations are stacking above 120K. I believe this level will inevitably be taken, provided we maintain bullish structure. Right now, we’re also testing the LTF downtrend line. If that breaks, it likely confirms a move toward at least 120K. Still, watch out for liquidity hunts.

Scenarios I’m watching:

1.) BTC maintains bullish structure and trends toward the previous weekly open above 120K.

2.) BTC loses bullish structure, forms a lower high, and drops below 115,700, opening the door to 110–112K.

At the moment, scenario 1 seems most likely. There are no clear signs of bearish continuation. We’ll either hold bullish structure or bearish signals will appear next week.

My LTF target is 120K. However, we could see a wick down early in the week (Monday or Tuesday) to clear longs before pushing higher. Watch for higher highs and higher lows to validate the move upward; otherwise, scenario 2 comes into play.

$BTC Analaysis

Lets keep it simple. BTC is currently in the bottom half of the range. Sandwiched between Monthly open and the untested weekend lows. Right now its 50/50. Here is what I am looking at:

Firstly, monday we tested the monthly open an rejected back down to 112600. As of now, we are bouncing. Either BTC forms a higher low an pushes back above the monthly open confirming continuation or... it remains below the monthly open & we sweep the weekend lows. 

Either way, you are either looking for triggers below the weekend lows or triggers above the monthly open sweeps. 

I'm considering two possible scenarios:

1.) Bitcoin dips below the weekend lows and then quickly reverses.

2.) Bitcoin forms a higher low front running weekend lows then breaks above the monthly open to continue moving higher.

If Scenario 2 plays out, monitor the LTF structure for continuation longs. If Scenario 1 unfolds, wait for price to reclaim and hold above the range lows before looking for entry triggers.

Hopefully this helps you understand my thought process.

A message from yours truly, Killaxbt

I see so many people step into trading with the wrong mindset. The first thought that crosses their minds is, “This is my chance to hit the jackpot and get rich overnight.”  

But for me, it was never about chasing fast money. 

From day one, it was about something far more important... freedom. The freedom to be your own boss. The freedom to choose how you spend your time. Because at the end of the day, time is your most valuable asset — and you can never get it back.

We live in a world that trains you from birth to be a cog in the machine. Go to school. Get a job. Work until you’re 65. Hope you have enough left in the tank to enjoy life.

That’s not living — that’s surviving. That’s the Matrix.

Most new traders fall into the trap of being money-hungry. But if you’re chasing fast money, you’ve already lost. In reality, the early stages should be about:

    •    Preserving capital
    •    Building a system
    •    Mastering risk
    •    Sharpening discipline

Your mindset shouldn’t be “How much money can I make today?” but rather, “What is the highest quality trade I can take?” 

When you focus on process over profit, the money becomes a byproduct of doing things right.

If you can control your emotions, stick to your edge, and stop treating trading like a lottery ticket — then slowly, brick by brick, you start building your escape.

This isn’t about getting rich quick.
It’s about reclaiming your time.
It’s about waking up.

If you stay disciplined and chase freedom over dollars, the money will follow — and you will finally beat the system. $BTC

$BTC | Weekly Analysis Report

Currently, BTC closed the weekly candle strongly, and the CME gap aligns closely with the previous weekly open. This area will likely serve as a key level for Continuation. There's significant liquidity sitting around 121K, which is also likely to be targeted.

Here’s how I see things shaping up on the LTF:

Scenario 1: 
Early Week Liquidity Sweep of Late Longs

We could begin the week by flushing out late longs. If that happens, I’ll be watching for a HL to form, ideally after the CME gap is filled near the prior weekly open. If that level holds, a bounce is likely, especially since BTC has already swept the 115K lows for liquidity. At this stage, the market is now seeking liquidity above.

Due to the strong weekly close, late longs might get trapped. If we see rejection at local resistance around 119,300–119,700, I’ll be looking for short setups back down toward the CME gap area. The gap this time is slightly larger than previous ones, which is why the initial entry might have been a bit early—hence the smaller position size.

Scenario 2: 
Early Week Push into 120–121K Liquidity

Alternatively, we could see an early push to grab the liquidity around 120–121K at the start of the week. In that case, scalp shorts become attractive due to favorable risk/reward. However, keep in mind the broader trend remains bullish, so any counter-trend trades should be kept lighter.

If we first run the wick highs into 121K, I’ll be eyeing short entries. That said, I still strongly believe the CME gap will eventually fill. Just pay close attention to 119,300–119,700, if weekness presents on the LTF I will short. If not - next area is around 121K.

$BTC | Weekly Analysis Report

Bitcoin is currently trading within a defined range, with $123K as the swing high and $115K as the swing low. Heading into next week, my focus shifts toward potential downside setups, especially around key liquidity areas.

Although there’s a CME gap below, which remains a high-probability target, I’m more interested in a sweep of the weekly low area. This region is likely to trigger a flush of stop-losses, tap into untested lows, and create liquidity — all of which align with a market structure that currently feels more bearish than bullish.

🔻 Bearish Scenario:
Break below the CME gap sets up a move toward the weekly wick low. I’d expect a deviation around that wick, with key confirmation being a reclaim of the range low on LTF.

My ideal long setup is a sweep below the wick low into the imbalance zone between 112K–114K. I’d likely start scalping around the weekly wick low and build into the trade if we hold the zone.

🟢 Bullish Scenario:
A reclaim of the weekly open at 119.2K flips short-term structure back to bullish. That opens the door for a push toward a new all-time high, invalidating the lower targets.

Most Favored Play:
My primary expectation is for BTC to sweep the 114K–115K lows, forming a mid-range deviation into my ideal long zone around 112K–114K. This would provide a high-probability setup with clean risk and clear invalidation.

Until the weekly open is reclaimed, I'm favoring a deeper liquidity grab to the downside. But if that low gets swept cleanly with signs of strength on the LTFs, I’ll be looking to step in with precision.

As outlined in my plan from a few months ago, we've now reached my target zone—hence my current caution. I’m not turning overly bullish here.

December Prediction
Back in December, when Bitcoin was at 108K, I was openly bearish and called for a move down to 80K. Three months later, that played out exactly as predicted.

March Prediction
In March, I stated that the market was bottoming and that “tariffs” were simply a scare tactic before the next major move. I posted my spot buys and swing entries, and shortly after, the market followed through.
I had another X account with more cycle predictions, but I deactivated it after creating this one—so I won't reference calls that can't be validated.

Throughout this cycle, I’ve consistently nailed nearly every high time frame (HTF) movement. Despite the hate, FUD, and noise, I’ve been right more often than not. So, what's my current outlook?
I believe the market will top in Q4. In my current plan, I’ve marked with a red dot where I expect the BTC cycle peak. While price predictions are inherently difficult, timing is where the real alpha lies. I expect some upside continuation into August/September before a meaningful correction.

Because of that, I won’t be entering swing shorts until I see proper confirmation of a breakdown. Predictions and ideas mean little without structural validation. That’s why I always wait for a confirmed shift in structure before acting, using my HTF view to validate entries.

You’ll know when I start building shorts. That time is approaching—but it’s not here yet. Stay patient amidst the noise. Don’t confuse caution with cluelessness. I may look foolish now, but a year from now, I won’t. And those who doubted? They’ll be gone from my profile.

$BTC | I sold 50%. The question is why?

First, some context: I bought my $BTC back in 2023 around $30K, and added more on the dips at $54K and $80K to maximize gains in what has remained a bull market. Having been in the market for six years, I’ve learned some hard lessons—chief among them is that greed often leads to exiting positions too late.

With that in mind, I’d rather exit early than too late. Sure, I could miss out on some additional upside, but I refuse to stay trapped in the mindset of always calling for higher when macro indicators on the weekly and monthly timeframes are starting to shift. I fully recognize we’re in a blow-off top phase, and the market can stay irrational longer than most expect. That’s exactly why I haven’t posted any shorts or new longs—I’ve mostly been sitting on my hands, letting the remainder of my spot holdings work passively.

Last cycle, I made the mistake of trying to time everything perfectly. This cycle, I’m not aiming for perfection. I’m simply looking to exit where I believe the price is approaching a top based on macro signals. Yes, BTC could still extend toward 140–150K—but it could also drop 20% in a single candle. That’s a risk I’m unwilling to take, sacrificing realized profits for what I believe would be the last marginal gains of this cycle.

We’ve had a fantastic run. But based on my analysis, people consistently underestimate the strength of the dollar. Hedge funds and investors are buying BTC in anticipation of an inevitable fiat collapse, which could eventually lead to a true supercycle. However, timing the decline of the USD is close to impossible. The pattern has been clear: prolonged periods of printing, followed by global liquidity tightening as the dollar strengthens.

At the moment, the USD is sitting on a 13-year trendline. We could still retest the 94 region in the coming months before liquidity meaningfully contracts. When that happens, it will likely have an immediate impact on BTC’s price. Smart players scale out gradually—they don’t wait until it’s too late.

From a macro perspective on the weekly and monthly charts, BTC’s upside appears limited unless the USD collapses, which, again, is unpredictable. If the DXY follows the same pattern we’ve seen since 2012, BTC will eventually retrace—potentially with a 50–60% correction.

I trade based on the information in front of me, not the speculation that “this cycle is different.” One day, it will be—but that’s impossible to predict with any precision.

Just because I’m cautious and slightly bearish on the HTF doesn’t mean I’m blindly shorting every move. I’m not stupid. I’ll wait for HTF shifts and confirmations before considering any swing shorts. But as you know, this is X—people love to assume otherwise. When I start shorting, you’ll know. Now is not that time. My priority is capital preservation above all else.

I see the dynamics gradually shifting, and that’s my cue to begin scaling out. I won’t be a permanent bull just to drive engagement. 

Hopefully this gives you a clear picture of my outlook and expectations. As always, I can be wrong and invalidated—but most importantly, I will never deviate from my plan. It’s not bias; it’s the foundation of my strategy. Every time I’ve strayed from that, I’ve lost.

If you found this post helpful, please drop a like. Took quite a while to put together this chart.

$BTC | If everyone is waiting for a dip, you're already too late.  

You need to be positioned before the impulsive move — not chasing it after it happens. Remember, the market is engineered to exploit your emotions and perceptions, turning your reactions into its advantage.

Algorithms don’t just track price—they’re engineered to decode human emotion. They analyze fear, greed, hope, and hesitation in real time, recognizing not just what traders do, but why they do it. Every trade, every hesitation, every emotional reaction becomes data. These systems are built to detect shifts in sentiment before you're even aware of them. By the time you feel fear or FOMO, the algorithm has already adjusted, positioned, and priced it in. In this game, emotion isn’t noise—it’s a signal. And to the algo, that signal is pure opportunity.

If you missed the move, don’t worry. There will always be another opportunity. Chasing is what turns mistakes into disasters. Stay patient.

My primary target for the cycle climax has been, and remains, $120K. However, due to the accelerating momentum in BTC's price action, there’s now a  possibility that we may go higher than I initial projected. 

This isn’t a case of irrational "hopium" — it’s a measured observation rooted in momentum dynamics and my time based market analysis.

As many of you are aware, I operate predominantly on the HTF, where my consistency has proven itself countless times. My methodology focuses not on reactive noise but on structural patterns that tend to repeat over extended periods.

Bitcoin operates within a highly algorithmic structure — a repeating series of fractals and cyclical behaviors. While it’s often said that "history doesn’t repeat, but it rhymes," Since its creation, BTC has consistently followed a clear and repeating cycle pattern. These mechanics are not speculative; they’re observable.

From ATH to ATH, historical cycles have spanned roughly 1,400 days. If you refer to the visual I’ve shared, it clearly illustrates how BTC is approaching what I believe to be a critical pivot point — potentially within the next 2–3 months. 

What we are witnessing now is characteristic of a terminal parabolic phase, a "blow-off top" structure. While pinpointing the exact top from a price-based perspective is very difficult, identifying it through temporal analysis is demonstrably feasible — and I’ve substantiated this approach over time. Observing time based analysis vs price based is more accurate. Price based analysis is flawed and time based analysis is more accurate, hence why my predictions are mostly in dates not times.

During these euphoric phases, you'll inevitably hear narratives such as "this time is different," "supercycle theory," and "the end of bear markets." These are symptoms of late-cycle psychology, where participants unknowingly serve as exit liquidity for more strategic players.

My outlook isn’t based on sentiment — it's grounded in data. The math, the patterns, and the cyclical structure all point toward a macro top forming in Q4. That’s not my opinion; it’s what the chart is signaling. For this reason, I’ve adopted a cautious posture — sitting in spot allocations from $80K and remaining patient.

Yes, I exited some of our swing long positions prematurely — a error in hindsight. But that’s part of the game. The broader thesis remains intact, and the analysis, in its core, was and still is correct.

$BTC | Thoughts for the Remainder of This Cycle

I’ve noticed some confusion around my current market perspective so I want to take this opportunity to clarify my thoughts and outline my outlook for the rest of the year.

Let’s start with some context.
Back in December 2024, I released a video predicting 106K as a local top, followed by a retracement to 80K. That thesis played out as expected. Additionally, I publicly stated that March would mark a local bottom, and I disclosed my spot entries around 78,500 as well as swing longs from that region.

These weren’t guesses, they were calculated moves based on my HTF analysis...that’s where I capitalize. My targets are never random; every level I share is supported by technicals.

Where We Are Now?
In my view, we are entering the final stages of this bull cycle. You can choose to trust my process or not—that’s your call. But I stick to my plan until it’s invalidated. I don’t play both sides of the fence on the HTF. If I’m bullish, I’m bullish. If I’m bearish, I’m bearish.

Right now? I’m neutral.
We’re in a complex part of the cycle where the market could realistically go either way. On LTF, there’s a slight lean toward more upside. On th HTF, I believe the risk-reward is skewed to the downside, and the time to aggressively chase longs has passed.

I’ve previously outlined 114K–116K as a potential final target based on TA. That’s not just speculation. I don’t throw out price targets for engagement—I trade what the charts show.

I do not believe we will exceed 125K this cycle, with 120K being a realistic peak before entering a broader bear market phase. I’m not swayed by narratives like "supercycle" or "altseasons." History tends to repeat itself—every single time regardless of people splurting nonsense.

So yes, on the monthly, I’m bearish. On the daily/weekly, there may still be bullish opportunity. I’m simply warning people against chasing these last 10–15% exit pumps as the drawdown can be 40-50% from current market price. 

We have a 4 year liquidity cycle. 1 bear year. 3 bull years. We are currently on the last bull year (approx 5 months) till the end of the year. Now is not the time to be bullish. Now is not the time to be calling for higher. Now is the time to be cautious, smart, safe, capital preservation - Waiting for the shifts in momentum/trend/dynamics & THEN executing depending on structure & market positioning. Do not mistake me for someone who doesn't know what they are doing. Its difficult to separate the real from the fake on X and that's because 90% of social media is fake.

Final Thoughts
Don’t confuse my caution with indecision. I have both HTF and LTF plans, and all are backed by technical analysis—not directional bias. Stay sharp, stay patient, and understand where we are in the broader cycle.

Institutions/whales do not want you to know this. 

The smart Cross Margin Optimization Strategy. 

To effectively apply this strategy, you must possess a comprehensive understanding of market cycles—including the emotional phases that drive market movement. 

This requires advanced insight into the psychology behind each phase, as well as the technical aspects of accumulation and distribution techniques.

These bigger players utilize cross margin (Full Balance) to take high conviction trades around certain areas to maximum their gains. With the correct knowledge & market experience, this is how you quickly compound a account quickly. Buy understanding the market dynamics, you select the leverage based on the invalidation price. 

When price is trading at range highs or range lows, these bigger players assess the statistical probability of breaking vs holding the range. Based on the most probable outcome, they then select 5X-10X-20X Cross margin. This approach allows institutions to sidestep scam wicks and place liquidation levels in areas that are unlikely to be triggered, as long as the range remains intact. In other forms when done correctly, this strategy is called god mode. 

In most cases, within daily ranges, it’s possible to buy the range lows and sell the range highs multiple times—often 3 to 4 times—before the range breaks. 

Experienced traders capitalize on this by using 5x, 10x, or even 20x leverage, placing their liquidation levels near invalidation zones. These are areas that would only be breached in the event of a significant structural trend shift.

This strategy often allows for 5–7 successful trades within the same range. After a few profitable cycles, these traders start to adapt—no longer bidding the same lows or selling the same highs—because they anticipate the range structure may soon break. So you need to understand the dynamic of market phases/ranges deeply.

This is a common method used by large or institutional players to grow their balance strategically. It's important to note that institutions typically allocate only 5–10% of their total asset value to this strategy, rather than committing their full capital. $BTC

Liquidity/Market Structure Lesson – $BTC

It’s important to understand the difference between internal and external liquidity within a price range.

Internal Liquidity: Found inside the current trading range.

External Liquidity: Found outside the range—above resistance or below support.

Internal liquidity helps you read LTF price action and market structure. Since the market spends around 75% of the time ranging (not trending), getting a grip on this concept will help you navigate most trading environments.

Market makers LOVE internal liquidity because it's perfect for trapping traders. They use the middle of the range to bait longs or shorts depending on whether the structure is shifting up or down. When I’m trading within these internal ranges, I reduce my risk. I save my higher-conviction trades for when price breaks out and hunts external liquidity.

External liquidity grabs are key. They usually happen during emotional extremes—panic selling or euphoric buying. The first time price returns to these zones (after a trend starts), it respects them about 95% of the time. That’s when I capitalize.

To sum it up:
Use internal liquidity for scalps and reading LTF price structure.

Use external liquidity to catch larger moves and swing trades (when retails emotions are peak despair/euphoria).

$BTC | Weekly Analysis

We’re sitting in what most would call an “equilibrium” — middle range which is not giving a clear sense of direction. Some label it “indecision,” but is it really? Or is that just how we interpret messy candles when direction becomes hard to gauge?

Personally, I think these whipsaws are intentional - designed to confuse, mislead, and trap. So here's my thought process and how I'm viewing it:

Last week’s candle swept the 100K level, wiping out $750M in longs. Then, as those longs got liquidated, price pushed up and swept 110.7K — the previous weekly high. That move obviously wasn’t random. We nuked longs, ripped through shorts, then baited breakout longers before reversing again, liquidating another $1B on this drop to 102.5.

I like to treat scenarios like formulas. It helps me map out sentiment flow.

Longs taken > Shorts taken > Longs taken = Highest probability outcome forming.

You can frame this two ways:

1.) We trapped longs around 110.7K, printed a swing fail, and now we can expect a corrective wave. Or..

2.) We baited breakout longers, absorbed shorts on the retrace, and now the market is questioning whether those 100K lows still hold.

Both directions have logic behind them. Here's where I stand:

Scenario 1: Weekly open starts red, forms a new low, then reverts mid-range for the rest of the week.
Scenario 2: Weekly open pushes up, tags 50% of the current candle (if we close neutral), then rolls over back to range lows.

Bottom line: we’re still range-bound. The lows may hold — at worst we see deviation. I’m seeing a lot of corrective pattern calls, but nothing structurally justifies a macro correction yet. This is chop. Controlled chop. Hunt high leverage - Price pumps to bait longs, dumps to liquidate them, sparks short FOMO — then the cycle repeats.

Remember, behind every movement there is a reason, in this case its "war" but it wasn't the war which done anything. It was simply hunting liquidity and it was priced in on the chart. After liquidity is exhausted on one side, price seeks inefficiencies in the opposite direction.

Hopefully this post helps! We are range bound until proven otherwise & that is how you should trade this movement.

$BTC | Analysis – Key Levels & Scenarios

So, BTC recently pulled back 10% from its ATH at $112K, testing the $100K level for the first time. This kind of correction is healthy in an uptrend and doesn't change my bullish bias — but as always, it’s important to consider multiple scenarios, not just the ideal one.

Why We Bounced from $100K
The bounce from $100K wasn’t random — it happened for three key reasons:

Daily FVG and Volume Imbalance: Price filled key inefficiencies on the chart.

Liquidity Sweep: We took out previous weekly lows, which means stop-losses got triggered — grabbing liquidity for a reversal (I highlighted this as a likely scenario in my last analysis, especially with the new monthly open)

Short Squeeze Setup: Over $1B in longs were liquidated during the drop. Once price bounced, funding went negative, signaling a short-heavy market. This helped fuel a strong rally as shorts started covering with twap bid.

Current Resistance: 104800-106000
We’re now testing a key resistance area — the 0.5 to 0.618 retracement of the recent drop.

If we break and hold above this zone → shorts are trapped, and price likely pushes to new highs.
(Scenario 1 – Bullish continuation)

If we get rejected here → we likely head back down to retest $100K.
(Scenario 2 – Short-term pullback)

Worst-case scenario: 
We break below $100K and start testing deeper support zones — the mid-range of the entire bullish leg (also around the 0.5–0.618 zone of the full move).
(Scenario 3 – Deeper correction)

My View

Funding is still flashing negative — showing traders are still leaning short (which can fuel upside).
Premium index is also negative — another contrarian bullish sign.

The bounce was sharp and caught most off guard — no real "safe" long entry has been offered yet.
If I were a market maker, I'd keep pushing price up from here to trap more shorts and leave sidelined bulls chasing. 

Either way - I hope this update is useful, have a good weekend!

$BTC | Analysis 

Bitcoin is currently pulling back into the monthly close — so what can we expect the next couple of weeks?

Well, when you break it down into scenarios, the outlook becomes clearer:

The Monthly Close Matters
How $BTC closes this month will give us strong clues about what could unfold next.

Scenario 1 
BTC dumps into monthly close. The following monthly candle opens & sweeps previous areas of demand (either 102600 or 100800) within the first 4-5 days of a new monthly open.

• If scenario 1 occurs, we are likely going to have a very bullish June.

Scenario 2 
BTC reverses towards the upside into monthly close. The next monthly candle sweeps current ATH and deviates back below (all within the first 4-5 days of a new monthly open.   

• If scenario 2 occurs, we are likely going to chop/consolidate or mild bearish.

Don’t gamble on which scenario will play out. Instead, observe and respond accordingly.

If we see an early-month dump, a reversal higher and a green close becomes probable.

If price opens strong and holds above $112K (ATH), the bullish trend may continue.

When simplified, we’re essentially dealing with three possible outcomes, each with roughly equal probability (~33.3%). Be reactive, not predictive — the edge is in the reaction, not the assumption.

Liquidity Heatmaps

I know alot of traders rely on platforms like @coinglass_com — and for good reason, it's a powerful tool. 

But it’s worth asking: 
If everyone has access to the same data, how reliable is it?

I’ve been using liquidity heatmaps for some time now, and I’ve found them to be quite valuable — but only when combined with a solid technical foundation and other confluences.

The key is understanding how liquidity concepts interact with market behavior. While everyone might see the same heatmap data, not everyone will act on it the same way. Emotions like fear and doubt can cloud judgment. In contrast, a trader with experience and a well-defined strategy is more likely to execute effectively when the time comes.

You’ll often see these heatmaps shared around, but here's the truth: when backed by strong technical analysis, they can significantly increase the probability of price testing those levels, making them solid entry zones in many cases.

Most importantly, if you understand higher timeframe (HTF) market structure, positioning, and trend, HTF liquidation heatmaps can be highly accurate. They shouldn't be overlooked.

In summary, heatmaps are a subtle but useful confluence in both LTF and HTF analysis — especially when estimating liquidation zones.

Reality Check: Most Traders Don’t Win 

Here’s the hard truth—not everyone makes money in the markets. While traders strive to remain rational, markets often behave irrationally. Many remain fixated on outdated strategies like "buy low, sell high," only to get caught off guard when market dynamics shift.

Adaptability is what separates survivors from casualties.

During a bull run, the paradigm shifts—it’s no longer about buying the dip, but about buying strength and selling into momentum. This isn’t greed; it’s simply understanding how trends behave. In fast, impulsive markets, textbook pullbacks and perfect entries rarely appear. Waiting for the ideal setup often means getting left behind.

Worse still, some traders fight the trend—shorting strength without regard for the broader market structure or the current phase of the macro cycle. That’s a recipe for consistent losses.

If there’s one lesson to internalize, it’s this:
The trend is your friend—until it isn’t.

And knowing when that changes?
That’s where I come in. 😉

Listen...

When the market is in a clear bullish trend, majority of traders often perceive the move as overextended. That perception triggers a COGNITIVE bias

Many traders are acustom to — longing "Support" and shorting "Resistance" purely based on the assumption that price is due for a corrective phase.

Yes, the statistical probability of a retracement increases after an extended impulse leg — but that doesn’t validate fading the trend. You’re still positioning against directional flow, structure, and momentum.

Just because a setup aligns with historical probability doesn’t mean it aligns with current market context or intent. A favorable R:R or textbook entry at a key level is irrelevant if it’s disconnected from the broader narrative.

In short: probability without context is noise. A setup that looks favorable in isolation is often invalid when viewed through the lens of trend continuity, market structure, and liquidity dynamics. 

So next time you’re about to short an impulsive leg or long a corrective wave, ask yourself:

Am I trading based on the market’s actual structure and momentum — or am I letting the bias “too high means correction, too low means pump” cloud my judgment and force me into a premature trade?

Retail call it "Resistance"  

MM call it Liquidity.

It's common for traders to expect a pullback after a strong impulsive move — and conceptually, they're not wrong. But more often than not, their timing is off. In a bull market, price action tends to defy logic, and so-called resistance levels act as liquidity. 

It’s not about being "Bulltarded"  — it’s about recognizing the specific phases of market structure, such as accumulation, mark-up, and distribution. Impulsive moves display similar characteristics, like shallow retracements, because their momentum is fueled by sidelined traders waiting for a deeper pullback that never comes.

Trying to short the "top" is like bringing a spoon to a sword fight. Adjust your line of thinking & don't be liquidity.

$BTC #BTC | Market Structure Update

1. Current Resistance Zone (94–95K):
Bitcoin is testing a major area of interest—this zone aligns with the high wick of the previous monthly candle and the daily session Point of Control (POC). It's a high-volume supply zone, which means sellers may begin to step in if demand doesn't show up in the short term. A rejection here would make the 90K region key for watching the formation of a potential higher low (HL).

2. Market Behavior & Psychology:
This recent impulse move feels designed to catch latecomers off guard—especially dip buyers waiting on the sidelines. As I mentioned in my post around 80K, the increase in M2 money supply and tariff uncertainties likely marked the bottom. I took a spot position back then (despite some losses on the short side recently). Reminder to self: trust your bias.

Scenario 1:
Price pulls back toward the previous monthly range lows (90–92K), forms a higher low, and then resumes its upward trajectory toward 100K. This could be the final retest we see if the trend remains intact.

Scenario 2:
BTC continues parabolically, breaking above the previous monthly wick high and accepting above the yearly range. This could trigger a short squeeze, potentially pushing price rapidly into the 98–99K area before a lower timeframe correction.

Alternate Pullback Scenario:
We may not need a deep correction to the full 90K range lows. Given the nature of this impulse, even a shallow pullback to 91–92K could be enough to reset momentum.

📌 Final Thoughts:

The current structure is undoubtedly bullish—no need to overcomplicate it. Just keep a close eye on these key levels. Whether you're a trend follower or a level-to-level trader, these structural zones will likely dictate the next major move.

How to effectively trade $BTC using Internal & External Liquidity

Internal Liquidity = Smaller levels inside Range
External Liquidity = Key Range Highs & Range Lows

Understanding the structural mechanics of market movements is crucial, as it aids in predicting future price action. Every break of structure, every HH/HL, Reclaim, all plays a important significance in regards to the direction of future trend. 

How to trade Internal Liquidity?
• Stop Loss based on realistic TP 
• Position in favour of trend (HTF & LTF)
• Analyze 1H/4H Significant levels only

How to trade External Liquidity?
• Prepare for multiple scam wicks, Scale slowly
• Stop loss slightly wider as hunts are expected
• Analyze LTF PA for reclaims/structure shifts

These dynamics are essential, as they enable you to take advantage of favorable trade opportunities based on price action formation.
